title: Chronic Pancreatitis
oneLiner: Chronic pancreatitis: progressive inflammatory fibrosis causing exocrine/endocrine insufficiency, malabsorption, pain, diabetes – high morbidity, increased cancer risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute pancreatitis (superimposed):** severe epigastric pain + vomiting + lipase >3x ULN → manage as acute pancreatitis (fluids, NBM, analgesia)
- **Ruptured pseudoaneurysm (e.g. gastroduodenal artery):** sudden severe epigastric pain + hypotension + falling Hb → immediate surgical/IR consult
- **Acute cholangitis (from CBD stricture):** fever + jaundice + RUQ pain → urgent ERCP
sections.Presentation:
- Epigastric pain (radiating to back, worse after meals)
- Steatorrhoea (foul, pale, oily, floats)
- Unexplained weight loss
- New-onset diabetes (type 3c)
- Exam: epigastric tenderness, cachexia, signs of malnutrition (angular stomatitis, glossitis), rarely erythema ab igne, palpable mass (pseudocyst)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Peptic ulcer disease** – pain relieved by antacids, negative lipase, endoscopy positive
- **Pancreatic cancer** – painless jaundice, raised CA19-9, mass on CT (can be painful)
- **Gallstone disease** – RUQ pain + Murphy’s sign, no steatorrhoea
- **Chronic mesenteric ischaemia** – postprandial pain, fear of eating, weight loss, no steatorrhoea
- **Splenic vein thrombosis** – splenomegaly, gastric varices, no pancreatic calcifications
- **Splitter:** Steatorrhoea + pancreatic calcifications on CT → chronic pancreatitis (not cancer)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose, urine dip (glucose, ketones)
- **Bloods:**
  - Lipase (to rule out acute pancreatitis) – may be normal or mildly elevated in chronic
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, HbA1c
  - Faecal elastase-1 (send on ward, result next day) – <200 µg/g = exocrine insufficiency
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT abdomen with contrast (calcifications, atrophy, dilated duct, exclude mass)
  - Second-line: MRCP/MRI (ductal anatomy), EUS (early changes)
- **Special tests:** 72-hour faecal fat collection (rarely needed)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Imaging (CT/MRI) showing calcifications/atrophy + typical pain + exocrine insufficiency (faecal elastase) – NICE CG104
- **Never-miss test:** CT abdomen with IV contrast to exclude pancreatic cancer and complications
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute on chronic → manage as acute pancreatitis (IV fluids, NBM, analgesia)
  - If stable → oral analgesia + dietary advice
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Pain control (paracetamol → NSAIDs → opiates PRN, but avoid long-term opiates)
  - Enzyme replacement with meals (Creon/pancreatin)
  - Monitor blood glucose; start insulin if diabetic (type 3c – oral agents less effective)
  - Nutritional support: low-fat diet, MCT oil, ADEK vitamin supplements
  - If jaundice/biliary obstruction → ERCP with stenting
  - Alcohol cessation counselling + smoking cessation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Pain management clinic
  - Repeat imaging (CT/MRI) for cancer surveillance (every 2-3 years if heavy risk)
  - Consider duct stenting or surgery (e.g. Puestow procedure) for obstructive pancreatitis
- **Default Orders (if admitted for acute flare):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline, start at 125 mL/hr (adjust for AKI)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if no bleeding
  - Diet: NBM if vomiting → clear fluids → light low-fat diet
  - Observations: 4-hourly (baseline, then as per NEWS2)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Pancreatic enzyme replacement:**
  - Creon 25,000 or 40,000 units per meal (start 25,000, titrate to 50,000)
  - Take WITH food (capsules whole, do not crush)
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS
  - If inadequate: ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (caution: avoid if GI bleed/peptic ulcer)
  - Breakthrough: morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (consider pain team if frequent opiates)
- **Diabetes:**
  - Usually insulin (e.g. basal-bolus) – oral agents often ineffective; [check local policy] for specific regimes
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs – avoid if eGFR <30
  - Morphine – caution in hepatic impairment (reduce dose)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Enzyme underdosing** – leads to ongoing malabsorption; but **overdosing** can cause perianal irritation and rarely hyperuricosuria. Biggest error: giving oral hypoglycaemics (e.g. metformin) in type 3c diabetes – high risk of hypoglycaemia.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pseudocyst (abdominal mass, infection, rupture)
  - Splenic vein thrombosis (splenomegaly, gastric varices)
  - Pseudoaneurysm (haemorrhage, sudden collapse)
  - Acute cholangitis (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)
- **Chronic:**
  - Steatorrhoea, malnutrition, weight loss
  - Type 3c diabetes (brittle)
  - Pancreatic cancer (5-10x increased risk)
  - Biliary stricture with obstructive jaundice
  - Osteoporosis (vitamin D malabsorption)
sections.Prognosis:
- 50% mortality at 20 years (due to malnutrition, infection, cancer)
- Pain improves over time but often chronic; quality of life poor
- Smoking cessation + alcohol cessation improve survival and reduce flares
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Stop alcohol and smoking** – single most effective intervention, discuss at every opportunity
2. **Check faecal elastase-1 before starting Creon** – confirms exocrine insufficiency (do not start blind)
3. **CT in any new diagnosis** – rule out pancreatic cancer, which can present identically
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Don't forget lipase in a flare** – may miss superimposed acute pancreatitis
2. **Don't prescribe opiates without a pain management plan** – high addiction risk; involve pain team early
3. **Don't dismiss new diabetes as type 2** – treat as type 3c (insulin usually required); avoid oral agents
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening pain not controlled by oral analgesia, vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, new jaundice, melaena/haematemesis
- **Follow-up:** Gastroenterology clinic in 2-4 weeks; dietitian referral; smoking cessation service
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >4** or **GCS <13** → call medical registrar
- **Signs of ruptured pseudoaneurysm** (hypotension + falling Hb) → call surgical/IR reg immediately
- **Acute cholangitis** (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) → call gastro for ERCP (within 24h)

rawMarkdown:
## Chronic Pancreatitis

**One-liner:** Chronic pancreatitis: progressive inflammatory fibrosis causing exocrine/endocrine insufficiency, malabsorption, pain, diabetes – high morbidity, increased cancer risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute pancreatitis (superimposed):** severe epigastric pain + vomiting + lipase >3x ULN → manage as acute pancreatitis (fluids, NBM, analgesia)
- **Ruptured pseudoaneurysm (e.g. gastroduodenal artery):** sudden severe epigastric pain + hypotension + falling Hb → immediate surgical/IR consult
- **Acute cholangitis (from CBD stricture):** fever + jaundice + RUQ pain → urgent ERCP

### Presentation
- Epigastric pain (radiating to back, worse after meals)
- Steatorrhoea (foul, pale, oily, floats)
- Unexplained weight loss
- New-onset diabetes (type 3c)
- Exam: epigastric tenderness, cachexia, signs of malnutrition (angular stomatitis, glossitis), rarely erythema ab igne, palpable mass (pseudocyst)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Peptic ulcer disease** – pain relieved by antacids, negative lipase, endoscopy positive
- **Pancreatic cancer** – painless jaundice, raised CA19-9, mass on CT (can be painful)
- **Gallstone disease** – RUQ pain + Murphy’s sign, no steatorrhoea
- **Chronic mesenteric ischaemia** – postprandial pain, fear of eating, weight loss, no steatorrhoea
- **Splenic vein thrombosis** – splenomegaly, gastric varices, no pancreatic calcifications
- **Splitter:** Steatorrhoea + pancreatic calcifications on CT → chronic pancreatitis (not cancer)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose, urine dip (glucose, ketones)
- **Bloods:**
  - Lipase (to rule out acute pancreatitis) – may be normal or mildly elevated in chronic
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, HbA1c
  - Faecal elastase-1 (send on ward, result next day) – <200 µg/g = exocrine insufficiency
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT abdomen with contrast (calcifications, atrophy, dilated duct, exclude mass)
  - Second-line: MRCP/MRI (ductal anatomy), EUS (early changes)
- **Special tests:** 72-hour faecal fat collection (rarely needed)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Imaging (CT/MRI) showing calcifications/atrophy + typical pain + exocrine insufficiency (faecal elastase) – NICE CG104
- **Never-miss test:** CT abdomen with IV contrast to exclude pancreatic cancer and complications

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute on chronic → manage as acute pancreatitis (IV fluids, NBM, analgesia)
  - If stable → oral analgesia + dietary advice
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Pain control (paracetamol → NSAIDs → opiates PRN, but avoid long-term opiates)
  - Enzyme replacement with meals (Creon/pancreatin)
  - Monitor blood glucose; start insulin if diabetic (type 3c – oral agents less effective)
  - Nutritional support: low-fat diet, MCT oil, ADEK vitamin supplements
  - If jaundice/biliary obstruction → ERCP with stenting
  - Alcohol cessation counselling + smoking cessation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Pain management clinic
  - Repeat imaging (CT/MRI) for cancer surveillance (every 2-3 years if heavy risk)
  - Consider duct stenting or surgery (e.g. Puestow procedure) for obstructive pancreatitis
- **Default Orders (if admitted for acute flare):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline, start at 125 mL/hr (adjust for AKI)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if no bleeding
  - Diet: NBM if vomiting → clear fluids → light low-fat diet
  - Observations: 4-hourly (baseline, then as per NEWS2)

### Drugs & Doses
- **Pancreatic enzyme replacement:**
  - Creon 25,000 or 40,000 units per meal (start 25,000, titrate to 50,000)
  - Take WITH food (capsules whole, do not crush)
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS
  - If inadequate: ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (caution: avoid if GI bleed/peptic ulcer)
  - Breakthrough: morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (consider pain team if frequent opiates)
- **Diabetes:**
  - Usually insulin (e.g. basal-bolus) – oral agents often ineffective; [check local policy] for specific regimes
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs – avoid if eGFR <30
  - Morphine – caution in hepatic impairment (reduce dose)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Enzyme underdosing** – leads to ongoing malabsorption; but **overdosing** can cause perianal irritation and rarely hyperuricosuria. Biggest error: giving oral hypoglycaemics (e.g. metformin) in type 3c diabetes – high risk of hypoglycaemia.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pseudocyst (abdominal mass, infection, rupture)
  - Splenic vein thrombosis (splenomegaly, gastric varices)
  - Pseudoaneurysm (haemorrhage, sudden collapse)
  - Acute cholangitis (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)
- **Chronic:**
  - Steatorrhoea, malnutrition, weight loss
  - Type 3c diabetes (brittle)
  - Pancreatic cancer (5-10x increased risk)
  - Biliary stricture with obstructive jaundice
  - Osteoporosis (vitamin D malabsorption)

### Prognosis
- 50% mortality at 20 years (due to malnutrition, infection, cancer)
- Pain improves over time but often chronic; quality of life poor
- Smoking cessation + alcohol cessation improve survival and reduce flares

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Stop alcohol and smoking** – single most effective intervention, discuss at every opportunity
2. **Check faecal elastase-1 before starting Creon** – confirms exocrine insufficiency (do not start blind)
3. **CT in any new diagnosis** – rule out pancreatic cancer, which can present identically

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Don't forget lipase in a flare** – may miss superimposed acute pancreatitis
2. **Don't prescribe opiates without a pain management plan** – high addiction risk; involve pain team early
3. **Don't dismiss new diabetes as type 2** – treat as type 3c (insulin usually required); avoid oral agents

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening pain not controlled by oral analgesia, vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, new jaundice, melaena/haematemesis
- **Follow-up:** Gastroenterology clinic in 2-4 weeks; dietitian referral; smoking cessation service

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >4** or **GCS <13** → call medical registrar
- **Signs of ruptured pseudoaneurysm** (hypotension + falling Hb) → call surgical/IR reg immediately
- **Acute cholangitis** (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) → call gastro for ERCP (within 24h)
